</imports>

<findings>

[suggestion] [warning] Zone "PRD Proposal Pipeline" (packages-rex:prd-proposal-pipeline) has critical risk (score: 0.63, cohesion: 0.37, coupling: 0.63) — requires refactoring before new feature development

</findings>

<insights>

- Low cohesion (0.37) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
- High coupling (0.63) — 24 imports target "prd-domain-operations"
- 12 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- The proposal pipeline (propose → decompose → dedupe → diff → reconcile → acknowledge) forms a clear sequential processing chain; consider documenting this ordering to make the pipeline structure explicit
- Cohesion of 0.37 is below the healthy threshold, suggesting some files in this zone (e.g., canonical.ts, recommend/create-from-recommendations.ts) may belong in adjacent zones
- The 27 cross-imports from unit-analyze and 24 outbound to core create a dense dependency surface; the bidirectional flow between this zone and unit-analyze suggests the boundary between proposal generation and LLM orchestration is blurry
- Cohesion of 0.37 is below the 0.4 threshold, indicating mixed responsibilities — files from src/recommend/ and src/core/ pulled into this zone reduce its conceptual focus.
- Coupling of 0.63 exceeds the 0.6 warning threshold; the zone imports from and is imported by both unit-analyze and core, making it a high-traffic hub in the dependency graph.
- The guided.ts and acknowledge.ts services suggest an interactive proposal review flow; verify these are not accidentally duplicating logic already present in the chunked-review CLI commands in the cli zone.
- Zone "prd-proposal-pipeline" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- The prd-proposal-pipeline and llm-analysis-engine zones are co-located in src/analyze/ with no subdirectory separation — their boundary is invisible to filesystem navigation. A developer opening src/analyze/ sees files from both zones interleaved with no structural signal about which zone a file belongs to.
- The 12 entry points of this zone allow cli-mcp-interface to call internal pipeline steps directly, bypassing any intended sequential ordering (propose → decompose → dedupe → diff → reconcile → acknowledge). There is no enforced public surface that hides intermediate stages.
- prd-proposal-pipeline and llm-analysis-engine are both rooted in src/analyze/ with no subdirectory boundary — the zone split is a logical artifact of community detection, not a physical boundary. This makes the architectural separation fragile: any new file added to src/analyze/ will be assigned to one zone arbitrarily, and the 27+13 cross-imports will continue to grow as the directory grows.
- The wide entry-point surface (12 exports) of prd-proposal-pipeline means the sequential pipeline contract (propose → decompose → dedupe → reconcile) is unenforced — consumers can invoke any stage in any order. If pipeline stage ordering is a correctness requirement, the public surface should expose only the top-level entry (propose) and keep intermediate stages internal.
- prd-proposal-pipeline acts as a transit node inside a three-way cycle: llm-analysis-engine → prd-proposal-pipeline → prd-domain-operations, while prd-domain-operations has 11 reverse imports back into prd-proposal-pipeline — the zone is simultaneously a downstream consumer and an upstream dependency of the domain layer, making isolated unit testing require mocking in both directions at once
- The 2 reverse imports from prd-proposal-pipeline into cli-mcp-interface mean the pipeline implementation has compile-time visibility into CLI-layer concerns — a change to CLI command registration or interface types breaks the pipeline zone's build regardless of whether any pipeline logic changed
- prd-proposal-pipeline participates in two concurrent cycles (with llm-analysis-engine: 27+13, and with prd-domain-operations: 24+11) and additionally has 2 reverse imports into cli-mcp-interface — no single cycle can be resolved in isolation; all three cycles in the analyze sub-graph must be broken in a coordinated pass to restore a DAG structure
- The sequential pipeline contract (propose → decompose → dedupe → diff → reconcile → acknowledge) has no type-level enforcement — intermediate stage functions accept and return plain objects rather than branded types that would make out-of-order invocation a compile error rather than a runtime bug
- Introduce branded/nominal types (e.g. ProposedItem, DecomposedItem, DedupedItem) at each pipeline stage boundary to make stage ordering a compile-time constraint rather than an undocumented convention — this would let TypeScript catch out-of-order pipeline invocations that currently fail silently at runtime
- [call graph] 1010 internal calls, 105 outgoing, 40 incoming (cohesion: 0.91, coupling: 0.09)

</insights>
